Type 2 on oral agents — fasting and 2-hour post-meal periodically. CGM is increasingly accessible and offers more complete glucose pattern data. 8. Complications Microvascular Complications Diabetic Retinopathy: leading cause of new blindness in working-age adults. Non-proliferative (NPDR) → Proliferative (PDR) → maculopathy. Annual dilated eye exam mandatory. Treatment: laser photocoagulation, anti-VEGF injections (ranibizumab, bevacizumab). Diabetic Nephropathy: starts with microalbuminuria (30-300 mg/day), progresses to macroalbuminuria and declining GFR. Leading cause of end-stage renal disease in India.

starts with microalbuminuria (30-300 mg/day), progresses to macroalbuminuria and declining GFR. Leading cause of end-stage renal disease in India. Control BP, use ACE inhibitor/ARB, restrict protein intake, control glucose tightly. Diabetic Neuropathy: distal symmetric polyneuropathy (burning, tingling, numbness in stocking-and-glove distribution), autonomic neuropathy (gastroparesis, erectile dysfunction, orthostatic hypotension, cardiac autonomic neuropathy), mononeuropathies. Macrovascular Complications and Foot Care Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death in Type 2 DM. Annual 10-year CVD risk assessment. Use SGLT2 inhibitors or GLP-1 agonists with proven CV benefit in high-risk patients. High-intensity statin therapy for all over 40. Diabetic Foot: neuropathy + ischemia +

or GLP-1 agonists with proven CV benefit in high-risk patients. High-intensity statin therapy for all over 40. Diabetic Foot: neuropathy + ischemia + infection = foot ulcer and amputation risk. Daily foot inspection. Proper footwear. Prompt treatment of even minor wounds. Refer to wound care specialist for non-healing ulcers. Multidisciplinary foot team reduces amputation rates by 50-80%. 9. Emergency Management DKA management (hospital): Fluid resuscitation — 0.9% NaCl 1L over first hour, then 250-500 ml/hr based on hydration. IV insulin infusion (0.1 units/kg/hr). Potassium replacement (never give insulin without checking potassium). Identify and treat precipitant. Monitor glucose hourly, electrolytes 2-4 hourly. HHS management: Slower fluid replacement (more volume, over 24-48 hrs).

and treat precipitant. Monitor glucose hourly, electrolytes 2-4 hourly. HHS management: Slower fluid replacement (more volume, over 24-48 hrs). Goal is gradual glucose reduction. Insulin only when fluids alone fail to reduce glucose adequately. High DVT risk — prophylactic anticoagulation. Hypoglycemia: Conscious patient — 15g fast-acting carbohydrate (5 glucose tablets, 150 ml juice, 3 teaspoons of sugar dissolved in water). Repeat in 15 min if still low. Follow with snack. Unconscious patient — do NOT give anything by mouth. IV dextrose 25-50 ml of 50% dextrose. IM/SC glucagon 1 mg if no IV access. 10. Prevention and Diabetes in India Type 1 DM currently has no known prevention. Type 2 DM can be largely prevented or delayed. The DPP (Diabetes Prevention Program) trial showed that

Type 1 DM currently has no known prevention. Type 2 DM can be largely prevented or delayed. The DPP (Diabetes Prevention Program) trial showed that intensive lifestyle modification (5-7% weight loss, 150 min/week moderate exercise) reduced progression from prediabetes to diabetes by 58% — more effective than metformin. India-specific challenges: high rates of thin-fat phenotype (metabolic obesity at normal BMI), early onset, rapid urbanization, cost of CGM and newer medications, limited access to dietitians and diabetes educators, and high proportion of undiagnosed diabetes. Government of India initiatives: National Programme for Prevention and Control of Cancer, Diabetes, Cardiovascular Diseases and Stroke (NPCDCS). Free metformin and basic insulin available at government hospitals.

and Control of Cancer, Diabetes, Cardiovascular Diseases and Stroke (NPCDCS). Free metformin and basic insulin available at government hospitals. Pradhan Mantri Jan Aushadhi Kendras offer affordable generic medications. The future of diabetes care in India lies in community screening programs, digital health interventions (apps for SMBG logging, teleconsultation), point-of-care HbA1c testing, and affordable CGM to improve glycemic outcomes at the population level.

First Aid and Medical Emergencies — Comprehensive Guide 1. Principles of First Aid First aid is the immediate assistance given to any person suffering a sudden illness or injury, with care provided to preserve life, prevent the condition from worsening, and promote recovery, until professional medical help arrives. The primary survey (DRABC): Danger (ensure scene is safe), Response (check consciousness), Airway (open and maintain), Breathing (check for breathing), Circulation (check pulse, control bleeding). Call for help early — 112 (India emergency number). Do not delay in calling. Activate EMS immediately for: cardiac arrest, severe breathing difficulty, uncontrolled bleeding, suspected spinal injury, unconsciousness, chest pain, stroke symptoms. Universal precautions: use gloves if

difficulty, uncontrolled bleeding, suspected spinal injury, unconsciousness, chest pain, stroke symptoms. Universal precautions: use gloves if available. If not, use clothing, bags, or other barriers when handling blood or body fluids. Wash hands thoroughly before and after providing care. 2. Cardiac Arrest and CPR Recognising cardiac arrest: person is unresponsive, not breathing normally (absent or gasping breaths — agonal breathing is NOT normal breathing). CPR technique (adult): place heel of hand on the centre of the chest (lower half of sternum). Place other hand on top, interlace fingers. Compress straight down 5-6 cm at a rate of 100-120 compressions per minute. Allow full chest recoil. Minimize interruptions (<10 seconds). Rescue breathing: tilt head, lift chin to open airway.

compressions per minute. Allow full chest recoil. Minimize interruptions (<10 seconds). Rescue breathing: tilt head, lift chin to open airway. Pinch nose. Give breath over 1 second, watch for chest rise. 30:2 ratio (30 compressions to 2 breaths). If untrained or unable to do rescue breathing — compression-only CPR is still highly effective. AED: turn on, attach pads (right upper chest below clavicle, left lower side below armpit), follow voice prompts. Stand clear during analysis and shock. Resume CPR immediately after shock. Continue CPR until: EMS arrives and takes over, person recovers (starts breathing normally and moves), a second responder or AED takes over, or you are too exhausted to continue safely. 3. Choking Mild choking: person can cough, breathe, and speak. Encourage forceful

or AED takes over, or you are too exhausted to continue safely. 3. Choking Mild choking: person can cough, breathe, and speak. Encourage forceful coughing. Do not interfere with back blows unless coughing fails. Severe choking (adult): cannot breathe, cough, or speak. Universal sign — hands clutching throat. Back blows: lean person forward, give 5 firm blows between shoulder blades with heel of hand. Check after each blow. Abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver): stand behind, make fist just above navel (below ribcage), grasp with other hand. Pull sharply inward and upward 5 times. Alternate with 5 back blows. Special situations: pregnant/obese — chest thrusts (centre of breastbone) instead of abdominal thrusts. Infant choking (under 1 year): 5 back blows face-down on your forearm, then 5

— chest thrusts (centre of breastbone) instead of abdominal thrusts. Infant choking (under 1 year): 5 back blows face-down on your forearm, then 5 chest thrusts face-up. Never abdominal thrusts in infants. If person becomes unconscious: lower to ground, call 112, start CPR (compressions may dislodge object). Each time you open airway before rescue breaths, look for visible object — remove only if clearly visible (never blind finger sweeps). 4. Severe Bleeding Direct pressure is the first and most important step. Apply firm continuous pressure to the wound with a clean cloth, dressing, or bandage. Maintain pressure for at least 10-15 minutes without lifting to check. If cloth soaks through, add more on top. Elevation: raise the injured limb above heart level to reduce blood flow to the

without lifting to check. If cloth soaks through, add more on top. Elevation: raise the injured limb above heart level to reduce blood flow to the wound. Continue direct pressure simultaneously. Wound packing: for deep wounds where direct pressure is difficult (groin, axilla, neck) — pack gauze firmly into wound and apply pressure. Haemostatic gauze (Celox, QuikClot) is superior to plain gauze if available. Tourniquet: for limb bleeding that cannot be controlled with direct pressure. Apply 5-7 cm (2-3 inches) above the wound (NOT over a joint). Tighten until bleeding stops. Note and write the time of application prominently. A tourniquet correctly applied should cause significant pain — a painless tourniquet is too loose. Do not remove in first aid setting. Assess for shock: pale/ashen

applied should cause significant pain — a painless tourniquet is too loose. Do not remove in first aid setting. Assess for shock: pale/ashen skin, cold clammy skin, rapid weak pulse, rapid breathing, confusion, thirst, nausea. Lay patient flat, elevate legs (unless spinal/chest injury), keep warm, give nothing by mouth, call 112. Blood loss of >750 ml causes early shock signs; >1500 ml causes severe shock. 5. Anaphylaxis (Severe Allergic Reaction) Anaphylaxis is a severe, potentially life-threatening systemic allergic reaction. Common triggers: foods (peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish, milk), medications (penicillin, aspirin, NSAIDs, contrast media), insect stings (bees, wasps), latex. Signs: skin — urticaria (hives), angioedema (facial/throat swelling), flushing; respiratory — stridor,

media), insect stings (bees, wasps), latex. Signs: skin — urticaria (hives), angioedema (facial/throat swelling), flushing; respiratory — stridor, wheeze, dyspnoea, hoarseness; cardiovascular — hypotension, rapid weak pulse, dizziness, syncope; GI — nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps. Epinephrine (adrenaline) auto-injector (EpiPen): inject into outer mid-thigh (can be given through clothing). Standard dose: adult 0.3 mg (EpiPen), children 0.15 mg (EpiPen Jr). Hold for 10 seconds. Repeat after 5-15 minutes if no improvement (second dose if available). Position: if hypotensive/dizzy — supine with legs elevated. If breathing difficulty — sitting up. If unconscious — recovery position. Never leave standing (cardiovascular collapse risk). Call 112 immediately even if epinephrine given (effects

up. If unconscious — recovery position. Never leave standing (cardiovascular collapse risk). Call 112 immediately even if epinephrine given (effects temporary, biphasic reaction may occur in 4-12 hours). Hospital observation for minimum 4-6 hours after anaphylaxis. Patients should be discharged with prescription for 2 EpiPens and anaphylaxis action plan. 6. Burns Burn assessment: Rule of Nines (adult) — head 9%, each arm 9%, chest 18%, back 18%, each leg 18%, perineum 1%. Palm (with fingers) = 1% body surface area (BSA). Children: Lund-Browder chart (larger head proportion). Burn depth: Superficial (1st degree) — redness, pain, intact skin (sunburn). Partial thickness (2nd degree) — blistering, wet/weeping, very painful. Full thickness (3rd degree) — charred/leathery/waxy, painless (nerve

Partial thickness (2nd degree) — blistering, wet/weeping, very painful. Full thickness (3rd degree) — charred/leathery/waxy, painless (nerve destruction). 4th degree — down to bone/tendon. First aid for burns: STOP the burning process (remove heat source, douse flames, remove burning clothing — not stuck fabric). COOL — cool running water for minimum 20 minutes within 3 hours of injury. Do NOT use ice, iced water, butter, toothpaste, oil, or yoghurt. COVER — wrap loosely in cling film (single layer), non-stick dressing, or clean plastic bag. Do NOT use fluffy cotton, towels, or adhesive dressings. Do not pop blisters. Remove jewellery near burned area before swelling. Seek emergency care for: burns >1% BSA in children or >5% BSA in adults, full-thickness burns, burns on

jewellery near burned area before swelling. Seek emergency care for: burns >1% BSA in children or >5% BSA in adults, full-thickness burns, burns on face/hands/feet/genitals/major joints, circumferential burns, inhalation injury (singed nasal hair, hoarse voice, carbonaceous sputum). Give paracetamol for pain. 7. Head Injury and Spinal Injury Minor head injury: brief loss of consciousness (<30 min), post-traumatic amnesia <24 hours, GCS 13-15 post-resuscitation. Observe for: worsening headache, vomiting, confusion, unequal pupils, weakness, CSF from nose/ear (indicates base of skull fracture). Red flags requiring immediate CT and/or admission: GCS <13, focal neurological deficit, post-traumatic seizure, suspected skull fracture (haematoma over mastoid — Battle's sign, periorbital haematoma

GCS <13, focal neurological deficit, post-traumatic seizure, suspected skull fracture (haematoma over mastoid — Battle's sign, periorbital haematoma — raccoon eyes), penetrating injury, coagulopathy. Suspected spinal injury: occurs with high-energy trauma (fall from height, MVA, diving injury), elderly low-energy falls. Precautions: do NOT move patient unnecessarily. Inline stabilization of head and neck. Log roll technique for turning. C-spine collar if available. Unconscious trauma patient: assume spinal injury until proven otherwise. Head-tilt/chin-lift is modified to jaw thrust to open airway while maintaining c-spine neutrality. Primary survey takes priority — an uncontrolled airway kills faster than a missed c-spine injury. 8. Fractures, Dislocations and Sprains Assessing a

Primary survey takes priority — an uncontrolled airway kills faster than a missed c-spine injury. 8. Fractures, Dislocations and Sprains Assessing a fracture: pain, swelling, deformity (angulation, shortening), bruising, inability to use the limb, crepitus (grating sound — do not deliberately elicit), neurovascular deficit (check pulse, sensation, capillary refill distal to fracture). First aid for fractures: immobilize the fracture in the position found. Improvised splint using any rigid material (rolled newspaper, wood plank). Pad well. Extend beyond the joints above and below the fracture. Ice pack (wrapped) for swelling. Elevate. Analgesia. Open (compound) fracture: bone pierces skin. Do NOT attempt to push bone back. Cover with sterile/clean dressing moistened with saline. Maintain

Open (compound) fracture: bone pierces skin. Do NOT attempt to push bone back. Cover with sterile/clean dressing moistened with saline. Maintain immobilization. High infection risk — requires urgent surgical debridement and IV antibiotics. Call 112. Dislocations: shoulder (most common), patella, finger, hip (high energy). Do NOT attempt reduction in field (risk of vascular/nerve injury). Immobilize in position of comfort. Neurovascular assessment every 15-30 min. Urgent hospital. Exception: austere environment dislocations by trained providers. Sprains: PRICE — Protection, Rest, Ice (20 min every 2-3 hours, wrapped), Compression (elastic bandage), Elevation. Grade 3 sprains (complete ligament rupture) need orthopaedic assessment. 9. Medical Emergencies Diabetic emergencies: Hypoglycemia —

Elevation. Grade 3 sprains (complete ligament rupture) need orthopaedic assessment. 9. Medical Emergencies Diabetic emergencies: Hypoglycemia — if conscious, give 15g glucose (juice, sugar, tablets). Unconscious — do NOT give by mouth, IV dextrose or IM glucagon, call 112. Hyperglycemia/DKA — unable to manage at home, hospital required. Seizures: protect from injury (clear area, cushion head, turn on side). Do NOT restrain, put anything in mouth, or give water/food. Time the seizure. Call 112 if: >5 minutes, second seizure without recovery, known non-epileptic, first seizure, post-ictal confusion prolonged, or occurs in water/pregnancy. Drowning: call 112. Remove from water if safe to do so. Check breathing. If not breathing — give 5 rescue breaths first (unlike land CPR), then 30:2 CPR.

call 112. Remove from water if safe to do so. Check breathing. If not breathing — give 5 rescue breaths first (unlike land CPR), then 30:2 CPR. Remove wet clothing, prevent hypothermia. All drowning survivors need hospital evaluation even if apparently recovered. Hypothermia: remove wet clothing, gently move to warm environment. Warm the core first (warm fluids, warm blankets centrally — armpits, groin, neck). Do NOT massage limbs vigorously (cold peripheral blood to core can drop core temperature further). Hypothermic cardiac arrest — CPR continues; 'not dead until warm and dead' (rewarming can restore circulation in severe hypothermia). Heat stroke: core temperature >40°C with neurological symptoms (confusion, seizures, loss of consciousness). Emergency cooling: cool water immersion or

stroke: core temperature >40°C with neurological symptoms (confusion, seizures, loss of consciousness). Emergency cooling: cool water immersion or evaporative cooling (wet sponge + fan). Ice packs to groin, armpits, neck. Target temp <38.5°C. Call 112 urgently. 10. Poison, Bites and Stings General poisoning: identify poison if possible (preserve container/packaging). Do NOT induce vomiting (contraindicated for corrosives, hydrocarbons, or altered consciousness). Call 112 or Poison Control (India: 1800-116-117). Activated charcoal (50g adult, 1g/kg child) within 1-2 hours of ingestion if person alert — in hospital setting. Snake bite: most deadly medical emergency in India. Stay calm, limit movement of affected limb (splint if possible). DO NOT: cut and suck, apply tourniquet, give

most deadly medical emergency in India. Stay calm, limit movement of affected limb (splint if possible). DO NOT: cut and suck, apply tourniquet, give traditional remedies, apply ice. Identify the snake if safe to do so (photograph at safe distance). Reach hospital within 1-2 hours. Anti-Snake Venom (ASV) is the definitive treatment. Scorpion sting: painful with localised symptoms. Red Scorpion (Mesobuthus tamulus) in India can cause autonomic storm — hypertension, tachycardia, sweating, pulmonary oedema, myocarditis. Hospital admission mandatory for Red Scorpion stings. Prazosin (alpha-blocker) is the specific treatment in Indian protocol. Scorpion antivenom available in endemic areas. Dog bite / Rabies prevention: wash wound vigorously with soap and water for 15 minutes. This single

Scorpion antivenom available in endemic areas. Dog bite / Rabies prevention: wash wound vigorously with soap and water for 15 minutes. This single measure reduces rabies transmission risk by 90%. Anti-Rabies Vaccine (ARV) — WHO Essen protocol: Days 0, 3, 7, 14, 28 (5 doses). Category III exposure (bite on head/neck/fingers, deep wound, multiple bites) also requires Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG) on Day 0. Rabies is 100% fatal once symptomatic — prevention is everything. Jellyfish sting: rinse with sea water (not fresh water — activates more stingers). Remove tentacles with tweezers or a card (do not use bare hands). Immerse in hot water (as hot as tolerable) for 20-45 minutes — inactivates venom. Apply lidocaine gel if available. Seek emergency care for anaphylaxis, cardiovascular symptoms,

Coronary Artery Disease and Heart Failure 1. Overview Coronary artery disease (CAD) is the leading cause of death globally, responsible for approximately 9 million deaths per year. It results from atherosclerosis — progressive narrowing of coronary arteries by lipid-laden plaques. Heart failure (HF) is a clinical syndrome where the heart cannot pump sufficient blood to meet the body's needs or can only do so at elevated filling pressures. CAD is the most common cause of heart failure, followed by hypertension and cardiomyopathy. India has one of the highest burdens of CAD, with disease occurring a decade earlier than in Western populations. Approximately 45% of acute MI deaths in India occur in people under 55 years of age. 2. Pathophysiology of Atherosclerosis Atherosclerosis begins with

45% of acute MI deaths in India occur in people under 55 years of age. 2. Pathophysiology of Atherosclerosis Atherosclerosis begins with endothelial dysfunction triggered by risk factors such as dyslipidemia, hypertension, diabetes, and smoking. LDL particles enter the intima of arterial walls, oxidize, and are engulfed by macrophages, forming foam cells — the core of the early fatty streak. Over decades, lipid-laden foam cells, smooth muscle cells, and fibrosis form a plaque covered by a fibrous cap. A stable plaque causes angina during exertion. A vulnerable plaque with a thin fibrous cap can rupture, exposing the lipid core and triggering platelet aggregation and thrombosis — causing acute coronary syndrome (ACS). Acute Coronary Syndromes (ACS) include: Unstable Angina (UA — chest pain

platelet aggregation and thrombosis — causing acute coronary syndrome (ACS). Acute Coronary Syndromes (ACS) include: Unstable Angina (UA — chest pain at rest or increasing frequency, no biomarker rise), NSTEMI (Non-ST elevation MI — biomarker rise without ST elevation), and STEMI (ST elevation MI — complete coronary occlusion, time-critical emergency). 3. Risk Factors for CAD Major modifiable: hypercholesterolemia (especially elevated LDL and low HDL), hypertension, diabetes mellitus, smoking, obesity, physical inactivity, unhealthy diet, and metabolic syndrome. Non-modifiable: age (men >45, women >55), male sex (women's risk increases post-menopause), family history of premature CAD (first-degree relative with MI before age 55 in men, 65 in women), and South Asian ethnicity. Emerging

family history of premature CAD (first-degree relative with MI before age 55 in men, 65 in women), and South Asian ethnicity. Emerging risk factors: elevated Lp(a) lipoprotein, high sensitivity CRP (hsCRP), homocysteine, air pollution exposure (India's urban areas have among the highest particulate matter levels globally), chronic kidney disease, HIV, autoimmune diseases. 4. Clinical Presentations Stable Angina: exertional chest pain (pressure, tightness, burning) in the substernal area, radiating to jaw, left arm, or back. Relieved by rest and sublingual nitrates within 1-5 minutes. Indicates significant but stable coronary stenosis. Unstable Angina/NSTEMI: angina at rest, new-onset angina, or crescendo angina. Requires urgent hospitalization. ECG may show ST depression or T-wave

Unstable Angina/NSTEMI: angina at rest, new-onset angina, or crescendo angina. Requires urgent hospitalization. ECG may show ST depression or T-wave changes. Troponin elevated in NSTEMI. STEMI: sudden onset severe crushing chest pain, diaphoresis, nausea, vomiting, dyspnea. ECG shows ST elevation in ≥2 contiguous leads or new LBBB. This is a time-critical emergency — 'Door-to-Balloon' time should be <90 minutes. Atypical presentations: women, elderly, and diabetics may present with atypical symptoms — fatigue, jaw pain, upper back pain, dyspnea, or nausea without chest pain. High clinical suspicion required. 5. Diagnosis and Investigations TestPurpose / Findings ECGST changes, Q waves, LBBB, arrhythmias Troponin I or T (high-sensitivity)Myocardial necrosis marker — elevated in MI Chest

TestPurpose / Findings ECGST changes, Q waves, LBBB, arrhythmias Troponin I or T (high-sensitivity)Myocardial necrosis marker — elevated in MI Chest X-rayCardiomegaly, pulmonary edema, pleural effusion EchocardiogramWall motion abnormalities, EF, valve disease Exercise Stress Test (TMT)Provoke ischemia in suspected stable CAD CT Coronary AngiographyNon-invasive coronary anatomy, calcium scoring Conventional Angiography (CAG)Gold standard; guides PCI/CABG decision BNP / NT-proBNPHeart failure diagnosis and monitoring 6. Medical Treatment of CAD Antiplatelet therapy: aspirin 75-100 mg daily (lifetime). After ACS: dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) — aspirin + ticagrelor (90 mg BD) or clopidogrel (75 mg OD) for 12 months for ACS; 1-6 months after elective PCI. Statins: high-intensity statin

— aspirin + ticagrelor (90 mg BD) or clopidogrel (75 mg OD) for 12 months for ACS; 1-6 months after elective PCI. Statins: high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40-80 mg or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg) for all CAD patients, regardless of baseline LDL. Target LDL <70 mg/dL (very high risk) or <55 mg/dL (extremely high risk, post-ACS). Consider ezetimibe or PCSK9 inhibitors if target not reached. Beta-blockers: metoprolol, carvedilol, bisoprolol — reduce heart rate, BP, and myocardial oxygen demand. Mandatory after MI. Continue indefinitely in stable CAD, especially with LV dysfunction. ACE inhibitors/ARBs: mandatory post-MI with reduced EF (<40%), diabetes, or hypertension. Reduce LV remodeling, mortality. Titrate to maximum tolerated dose. Nitrates: sublingual GTN for acute angina relief.

diabetes, or hypertension. Reduce LV remodeling, mortality. Titrate to maximum tolerated dose. Nitrates: sublingual GTN for acute angina relief. Long-acting nitrates (isosorbide mononitrate, isosorbide dinitrate) for angina prevention. Nitrate-free interval essential to prevent tolerance. 7. Revascularization Percutaneous Coronary Intervention (PCI): balloon angioplasty + coronary stent. Primary PCI (pPCI) is the preferred reperfusion strategy for STEMI within 90 minutes of first medical contact. Drug-eluting stents (DES) reduce restenosis rates. Dual antiplatelet therapy required for 6-12 months post-stenting. Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting (CABG): preferred for left main disease, 3-vessel disease, diabetes with multivessel disease, and when PCI is technically not feasible. Uses

Grafting (CABG): preferred for left main disease, 3-vessel disease, diabetes with multivessel disease, and when PCI is technically not feasible. Uses internal mammary artery (IMA) graft for best long-term patency (>90% at 10 years). Thrombolysis (fibrinolytic therapy): used for STEMI when pPCI is not available within 120 minutes. Streptokinase (less expensive) or tenecteplase (single bolus, more convenient). Should be administered within 12 hours of symptom onset. 'Pharmacoinvasive strategy' — thrombolyse then transfer for early angiography. 8. Heart Failure — Overview and Classification Heart failure is classified by ejection fraction (EF): HFrEF (EF <40%), HFmrEF (EF 40-49%), HFpEF (EF ≥50%). HFrEF has the most evidence-based treatment. HFpEF (common in hypertensive, diabetic, elderly

HFrEF (EF <40%), HFmrEF (EF 40-49%), HFpEF (EF ≥50%). HFrEF has the most evidence-based treatment. HFpEF (common in hypertensive, diabetic, elderly women) remains a therapeutic challenge. NYHA Functional Classification: Class I (no symptoms), Class II (mild symptoms with ordinary activity), Class III (symptoms with less than ordinary activity), Class IV (symptoms at rest). Common causes: CAD/post-MI (most common), hypertension, dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM), valvular heart disease (rheumatic heart disease in India), viral myocarditis, alcoholic cardiomyopathy, thyroid disease. 9. Heart Failure Treatment Four pillars of HFrEF treatment ('Fantastic Four'): ACE inhibitor/ARB/ARNI (sacubitril-valsartan) + Beta-blocker (carvedilol/bisoprolol/metoprolol XL) + Mineralocorticoid antagonist

('Fantastic Four'): ACE inhibitor/ARB/ARNI (sacubitril-valsartan) + Beta-blocker (carvedilol/bisoprolol/metoprolol XL) + Mineralocorticoid antagonist (spironolactone/eplerenone) + SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin). Diuretics: furosemide (loop diuretic) for symptom relief of fluid overload — the single most important drug for symptom control. Titrate dose to achieve dry weight (target JVP normal, no peripheral edema, no orthopnea). Does not improve mortality. Device therapy: ICD (implantable cardioverter-defibrillator) for EF <35% on optimal medical therapy, for primary prevention of sudden cardiac death. CRT (cardiac resynchronization therapy) for EF <35% with LBBB and NYHA III-IV. 10. Cardiac Emergencies — What to Do STEMI: call 112/911 immediately. Give aspirin 300 mg to

therapy) for EF <35% with LBBB and NYHA III-IV. 10. Cardiac Emergencies — What to Do STEMI: call 112/911 immediately. Give aspirin 300 mg to chew (if not already taking, not allergic, not bleeding). Keep patient at rest, calm, monitor consciousness. No food or water if consciousness deteriorating. Transport to PCI-capable centre urgently. Time = muscle: every 30 minutes of delay increases 1-year mortality by ~7.5%. Cardiac Arrest: shout for help. Call 112. Start CPR: 30 compressions (centre of chest, depth 5-6 cm, rate 100-120/min) then 2 rescue breaths. Minimize interruptions. Use AED as soon as available — turn on, follow voice prompts, deliver shock if advised. Continue CPR until AED says no shock needed and patient starts breathing, or until emergency services take over. Acute

deliver shock if advised. Continue CPR until AED says no shock needed and patient starts breathing, or until emergency services take over. Acute Pulmonary Oedema: sit patient up (legs dangling). Call 112. Give supplemental oxygen. IV furosemide 40-80 mg (in hospital). IV nitrates (nitroglycerin infusion). Non-invasive ventilation (CPAP/BiPAP) if SpO2 does not improve.

Hypertension (High Blood Pressure) 1. Overview and Definition Hypertension is a condition in which the force of blood against the artery walls is persistently too high. Blood pressure is expressed as two numbers: systolic (pressure when heart beats) over diastolic (pressure when heart rests). Normal blood pressure is below 120/80 mmHg. Hypertension is defined when readings consistently exceed 130/80 mmHg (AHA 2017 guidelines) or 140/90 mmHg (older WHO/Indian guidelines). It is classified as: Elevated (120-129/<80), Stage 1 (130-139/80-89), Stage 2 (≥140/≥90), and Hypertensive Crisis (>180/>120). Hypertension affects over 1.28 billion adults worldwide and is responsible for an estimated 10.4 million deaths per year. In India, approximately 28-29% of adults are hypertensive, and urban

worldwide and is responsible for an estimated 10.4 million deaths per year. In India, approximately 28-29% of adults are hypertensive, and urban prevalence can reach 33%. Despite its prevalence, nearly half of affected people are unaware of their condition. The condition is called the 'silent killer' because most people experience no symptoms even when blood pressure reaches dangerously high levels. This makes regular screening critically important, especially for those with risk factors. 2. Pathophysiology How Blood Pressure Is Regulated Blood pressure is maintained through a complex interplay of cardiac output, systemic vascular resistance, blood volume, and neurohumoral factors. The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) is a key regulator — when blood pressure drops, kidneys

blood volume, and neurohumoral factors. The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) is a key regulator — when blood pressure drops, kidneys release renin, which ultimately produces angiotensin II, a potent vasoconstrictor. The sympathetic nervous system also plays a major role. Stress, physical activity, and emotional arousal activate the sympathetic nervous system, raising heart rate and vascular resistance. Chronic activation can permanently elevate blood pressure. Baroreceptors in the aortic arch and carotid sinuses provide short-term feedback. Kidneys control long-term blood pressure by regulating sodium and water retention. Any disruption in these systems can result in sustained hypertension. Types of Hypertension Primary (essential) hypertension accounts for 90-95% of all cases.

in these systems can result in sustained hypertension. Types of Hypertension Primary (essential) hypertension accounts for 90-95% of all cases. It has no identifiable single cause but is associated with genetic predisposition, aging, obesity, high-sodium diet, sedentary lifestyle, and excessive alcohol intake. Secondary hypertension (5-10%) has an identifiable underlying cause including: chronic kidney disease, primary aldosteronism, renovascular disease (renal artery stenosis), obstructive sleep apnea, thyroid disorders, pheochromocytoma, coarctation of the aorta, and certain medications (NSAIDs, oral contraceptives, decongestants, stimulants). Isolated systolic hypertension (high systolic with normal diastolic) is common in older adults due to arterial stiffening. Masked hypertension is

Isolated systolic hypertension (high systolic with normal diastolic) is common in older adults due to arterial stiffening. Masked hypertension is normal in clinic but high at home. White coat hypertension is the reverse — high in clinic, normal elsewhere. 3. Risk Factors Non-modifiable risk factors include: age (risk increases significantly after 55 in men and 65 in women), family history of hypertension, race (higher prevalence in South Asian and African-descent populations), and chronic kidney disease. Modifiable risk factors include: obesity (BMI >30), physical inactivity, high-sodium diet (>5g salt/day), low potassium intake, excessive alcohol consumption (>2 drinks/day), smoking, chronic stress, sleep disorders, and use of certain medications. In India, rapid urbanization has

alcohol consumption (>2 drinks/day), smoking, chronic stress, sleep disorders, and use of certain medications. In India, rapid urbanization has increased hypertension risk through dietary changes (increased processed food consumption), sedentary jobs, stress, and reduced physical activity. Rural-urban migration is a significant contributing factor. 4. Symptoms and Clinical Presentation Most patients with hypertension are completely asymptomatic. This is precisely why routine blood pressure checks are important, as the first presentation can sometimes be a hypertensive crisis, heart attack, or stroke. When symptoms do occur, they may include: morning headaches (occipital region), nosebleeds (epistaxis), tinnitus (ringing in ears), visual disturbances or blurred vision, dizziness,

include: morning headaches (occipital region), nosebleeds (epistaxis), tinnitus (ringing in ears), visual disturbances or blurred vision, dizziness, palpitations, and fatigue. Hypertensive urgency is severely elevated blood pressure (>180/120) WITHOUT end-organ damage. Symptoms may include severe headache and anxiety but no neurological deficits or chest pain. Hypertensive emergency is severely elevated blood pressure WITH end-organ damage. This includes hypertensive encephalopathy (confusion, seizures), acute MI, unstable angina, aortic dissection, acute pulmonary edema, acute renal failure, or eclampsia in pregnancy. This is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate hospitalization. 5. Diagnosis and Monitoring Measurement Standards Blood pressure should be measured with a

emergency requiring immediate hospitalization. 5. Diagnosis and Monitoring Measurement Standards Blood pressure should be measured with a validated device after 5 minutes of rest, seated with back supported, feet flat on floor, and arm at heart level. Two measurements 1-2 minutes apart should be taken, and the average recorded. Measurements should be taken in both arms initially. Home blood pressure monitoring (HBPM) is recommended to confirm diagnosis and assess treatment. Morning (before medications) and evening readings over 7 days provide the most reliable average. Target for home readings is <135/85 mmHg. Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) — worn for 24 hours — is the gold standard for diagnosing white coat and masked hypertension. It also provides nocturnal readings; a lack

(ABPM) — worn for 24 hours — is the gold standard for diagnosing white coat and masked hypertension. It also provides nocturnal readings; a lack of nighttime dipping (non-dipper pattern) is associated with higher cardiovascular risk. Investigations Initial workup includes: urinalysis (protein, blood, glucose), serum creatinine and electrolytes, fasting blood glucose, fasting lipid profile, complete blood count, ECG, and fundoscopy. Additional tests for suspected secondary hypertension: renal ultrasound, plasma renin and aldosterone, 24-hour urine catecholamines (for pheochromocytoma), thyroid function tests, sleep study for sleep apnea. Target organ damage assessment: echocardiogram for LVH, urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio for renal involvement, carotid intima-media thickness (CIMT),

organ damage assessment: echocardiogram for LVH, urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio for renal involvement, carotid intima-media thickness (CIMT), and ankle-brachial index (ABI). 6. Treatment — Lifestyle Modifications The DASH diet (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) is the most evidence-based dietary intervention. It emphasizes fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins, and low-fat dairy while limiting saturated fats, red meat, sweets, and sodium. It can reduce systolic BP by 8-14 mmHg. Sodium restriction: limit dietary sodium to less than 2,300 mg/day (ideally 1,500 mg for most adults). This means reducing processed foods, pickles, papad, salty snacks, and restaurant meals. Even a moderate reduction of 1,000 mg/day can significantly lower blood pressure. Regular aerobic

pickles, papad, salty snacks, and restaurant meals. Even a moderate reduction of 1,000 mg/day can significantly lower blood pressure. Regular aerobic exercise — at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity activity per week (walking, swimming, cycling) — can reduce systolic BP by 5-8 mmHg. Isometric resistance exercise (wall squats, handgrip) has also shown meaningful BP reduction. Weight loss is one of the most effective lifestyle interventions — each kilogram of weight loss reduces systolic BP by approximately 1 mmHg. Achieving a healthy waist circumference (men <90 cm, women <80 cm for South Asians) is the target. Limit alcohol to no more than 1 drink/day for women and 2 drinks/day for men. Excessive alcohol is responsible for approximately 16% of hypertension cases worldwide. Quitting

than 1 drink/day for women and 2 drinks/day for men. Excessive alcohol is responsible for approximately 16% of hypertension cases worldwide. Quitting smoking does not directly lower blood pressure but dramatically reduces overall cardiovascular risk. Stress management through mindfulness meditation, yoga, deep breathing (pranayama), and adequate sleep (7-8 hours) can help lower blood pressure. Chronic psychological stress activates the sympathetic nervous system and RAAS system. 7. Pharmacological Treatment First-Line Medications ACE inhibitors (enalapril 5-40 mg, lisinopril 10-40 mg, ramipril 2.5-10 mg): block conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone secretion. Side effect: dry cough (10-15% of patients), angioedema (rare but serious).

I to angiotensin II, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone secretion. Side effect: dry cough (10-15% of patients), angioedema (rare but serious). Contraindicated in pregnancy. Angiotensin Receptor Blockers — ARBs (losartan 50-100 mg, telmisartan 40-80 mg, valsartan 80-320 mg): block angiotensin II receptors directly. Similar efficacy to ACE inhibitors but without the cough. Preferred when ACE inhibitor cough is problematic. Also contraindicated in pregnancy. Calcium Channel Blockers — CCBs (amlodipine 5-10 mg, felodipine 5-10 mg, nifedipine SR 30-60 mg): relax arterial smooth muscle, reducing vascular resistance. Dihydropyridine CCBs (amlodipine) are preferred for hypertension; non-dihydropyridines (verapamil, diltiazem) also reduce heart rate. Thiazide Diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide

are preferred for hypertension; non-dihydropyridines (verapamil, diltiazem) also reduce heart rate. Thiazide Diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide 12.5-25 mg, chlorthalidone 12.5-25 mg, indapamide 1.25-2.5 mg): reduce sodium and water retention. Chlorthalidone is preferred due to longer duration of action. Monitor for hypokalemia, hyperuricemia, and glucose intolerance. Second-Line and Special Situations Beta-blockers (metoprolol succinate 25-200 mg, atenolol 25-100 mg, bisoprolol 2.5-10 mg): reduce heart rate and cardiac output. Particularly useful when hypertension is accompanied by angina, post-MI, heart failure, or tachyarrhythmia. Not first-line for uncomplicated hypertension. Aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone 25-50 mg): effective add-on therapy, especially for resistant hypertension.

for uncomplicated hypertension. Aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone 25-50 mg): effective add-on therapy, especially for resistant hypertension. Also beneficial in heart failure. Monitor potassium closely — risk of hyperkalemia, especially with ACE inhibitors or ARBs. Alpha-blockers (doxazosin 1-8 mg): useful when hypertension accompanies benign prostatic hypertrophy. Central alpha-2 agonists (clonidine, methyldopa) — methyldopa is the drug of choice in hypertension during pregnancy. Resistant hypertension (BP >140/90 on 3 medications including a diuretic) affects about 10% of hypertensive patients. Requires full-dose triple therapy, medication adherence assessment, ruling out secondary causes, and possible specialist referral. 8. Hypertensive Emergency — Emergency Protocol

medication adherence assessment, ruling out secondary causes, and possible specialist referral. 8. Hypertensive Emergency — Emergency Protocol Hypertensive emergency requires immediate controlled blood pressure reduction in a monitored setting (ICU/HDU). The goal is NOT to normalize BP immediately, as rapid reduction can cause ischemia (watershed stroke, myocardial ischemia, acute tubular necrosis). Target: reduce mean arterial pressure (MAP) by no more than 20-25% in the first hour, then to 160/100-110 over 2-6 hours, and to normal over 24-48 hours (except aortic dissection where faster reduction is needed). IV medications used: labetalol (bolus 20 mg IV then infusion), nicardipine infusion, sodium nitroprusside infusion (ICU only, cyanide toxicity risk), hydralazine IV (preferred in

(bolus 20 mg IV then infusion), nicardipine infusion, sodium nitroprusside infusion (ICU only, cyanide toxicity risk), hydralazine IV (preferred in eclampsia), esmolol infusion (aortic dissection). Oral nifedipine (immediate release) is no longer recommended for hypertensive emergencies due to unpredictable BP drops. Sublingual captopril may be used for urgency (not emergency). Specific situations: aortic dissection — target SBP <120 with heart rate control using IV beta-blocker + vasodilator. Eclampsia — IV labetalol or hydralazine, magnesium sulfate for seizure prevention. Hypertensive encephalopathy — reduce BP gradually, avoid overshooting. 9. Special Populations Elderly patients (>65 years): Start low, go slow. Start with half the usual dose. Target SBP 130-139 in fit elderly,

9. Special Populations Elderly patients (>65 years): Start low, go slow. Start with half the usual dose. Target SBP 130-139 in fit elderly, 140-149 in frail elderly. CCBs and diuretics are preferred. Avoid orthostatic hypotension — always check standing BP. Diabetes and hypertension: ACE inhibitors or ARBs are preferred for their renal protective effects (reduce proteinuria). Target BP <130/80 mmHg. Avoid beta-blockers as first-line (can mask hypoglycemia symptoms). Chronic Kidney Disease: ACE inhibitors or ARBs are first-line regardless of diabetes status. Monitor creatinine and potassium closely. Loop diuretics (furosemide) preferred over thiazides when GFR <30. Pregnancy hypertension: Safe drugs include methyldopa, nifedipine, labetalol, and hydralazine. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and

over thiazides when GFR <30. Pregnancy hypertension: Safe drugs include methyldopa, nifedipine, labetalol, and hydralazine. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and direct renin inhibitors are absolutely contraindicated in all trimesters. Young patients and women of childbearing age: Evaluate for secondary causes. CCBs and beta-blockers are often preferred. Thiazides can affect fertility. 10. Long-Term Management and Prognosis Hypertension requires lifelong management. Uncontrolled hypertension significantly increases the risk of: stroke (7x), heart failure (3-4x), coronary artery disease (3x), chronic kidney disease (5x), peripheral artery disease, and retinopathy. With appropriate treatment, cardiovascular risk can be substantially reduced. Each 10 mmHg reduction in systolic BP reduces major

and retinopathy. With appropriate treatment, cardiovascular risk can be substantially reduced. Each 10 mmHg reduction in systolic BP reduces major cardiovascular events by ~20%, stroke by ~35%, and heart failure by ~40%. Medication adherence is the single greatest challenge in hypertension management. Pill burden, side effects, cost, and lack of symptoms all contribute to poor adherence. Once-daily combination pills (polypill approach) improve adherence significantly. Regular monitoring: BP at every clinic visit, renal function and electrolytes every 6-12 months, fasting lipids and glucose annually, ECG and urine albumin annually, fundoscopy every 1-2 years. Patient education is essential: Understanding that hypertension has no symptoms, the importance of taking medications even when

every 1-2 years. Patient education is essential: Understanding that hypertension has no symptoms, the importance of taking medications even when feeling well, DASH diet principles, home BP monitoring technique, and when to seek urgent care (BP >180/120 or any warning symptoms).

Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) 1. Overview and Epidemiology Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) is defined as abnormalities of kidney structure or function, present for more than 3 months, with implications for health. CKD affects approximately 10% of the global population — approximately 850 million people. In India, the estimated prevalence is around 800 per million population (pmp). CKD is largely silent until advanced stages. It is a major risk factor for cardiovascular disease — CKD patients are more likely to die of cardiovascular events than to progress to end-stage renal disease (ESRD). Globally, it is the 12th leading cause of death, and its burden is rising. In India, diabetes and hypertension together account for over 40% of CKD cases. Other important causes include chronic

and its burden is rising. In India, diabetes and hypertension together account for over 40% of CKD cases. Other important causes include chronic glomerulonephritis, obstructive uropathy (kidney stones, prostatic hypertrophy), renovascular disease, and 'CKD of unknown aetiology' (CKDu), particularly in farming communities (hot climate, pesticide and agrochemical exposure, dehydration). 2. G5<15Kidney failure (ESRD) — dialysis or transplant CKD is also classified by albuminuria (A1: <30 mg/g, A2: 30-300 mg/g microalbuminuria, A3: >300 mg/g macroalbuminuria). The CGA (Cause-GFR-Albuminuria) classification guides prognosis and management. 3. Causes and Risk Factors Leading causes: Diabetic nephropathy (most common in India and globally) — hyperglycemia damages glomerular capillaries, causing Kimmelstiel-Wilson lesions. Hypertensive nephrosclerosis — chronic high BP causes arteriosclerosis and ischemic nephron loss. Other causes: IgA nephropathy (most common primary GN worldwide), focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), membranous nephropathy, polycystic kidney disease (PKD — genetic, causing bilateral enlarged cystic kidneys), lupus nephritis, obstructive nephropathy. nephropathy, polycystic kidney disease (PKD — genetic, causing bilateral enlarged cystic kidneys), lupus nephritis, obstructive nephropathy. Risk factors for CKD progression: proteinuria (strongest modifiable predictor), uncontrolled hypertension, poor glycaemic control, smoking, NSAIDs (nephrotoxic — avoid in CKD), contrast media (acute-on-chronic), hyperuricaemia, anaemia, CKD-MBD (metabolic bone disease). 4. Symptoms and Complications CKD is often asymptomatic until Stage 4-5. Symptoms of uraemia (late-stage): fatigue, pruritus (itching — phosphate and uraemic toxin deposition), anorexia, nausea and vomiting, hiccups, metallic taste, oedema (legs, face), dyspnoea (fluid overload, anaemia, uraemic pleuritis), decreased urine output, confusion (uraemic encephalopathy). CKD complications:

(legs, face), dyspnoea (fluid overload, anaemia, uraemic pleuritis), decreased urine output, confusion (uraemic encephalopathy). CKD complications: Anaemia of CKD — EPO deficiency + functional iron deficiency. Metabolic bone disease (CKD-MBD): hyperphosphataemia, hypocalcaemia, secondary hyperparathyroidism (high PTH) → renal osteodystrophy, vascular calcification. Metabolic acidosis. Hyperkalaemia (life-threatening cardiac arrhythmia risk). Fluid overload (hypertension, heart failure). Uraemic pericarditis. 5. Cystatin C-based eGFR more accurate especially at GFR 45-60. Urine: urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR) — morning spot

equation preferred). Cystatin C-based eGFR more accurate especially at GFR 45-60. Urine: urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR) — morning spot urine. Urine dipstick for haematuria, leucocytes. Urine protein-creatinine ratio (PCR). Imaging: renal ultrasound — kidney size (small kidneys = chronic, large = polycystic, infiltrative, or acute-on-chronic), echogenicity, cysts, stones, hydronephrosis. Blood tests: U&E; (urea, electrolytes, creatinine), bicarbonate, haemoglobin, calcium, phosphate, PTH, uric acid, fasting glucose, HbA1c, lipid profile, hepatitis B/C, HIV screening before transplant. 6. Management of CKD Treating underlying cause: optimise glycaemic control (HbA1c <7.0%), blood pressure control (target <130/80 mmHg — use ACE inhibitor or ARB as first-line for their

cause: optimise glycaemic control (HbA1c <7.0%), blood pressure control (target <130/80 mmHg — use ACE inhibitor or ARB as first-line for their nephroprotective effects, especially in proteinuric CKD). Reduce proteinuria: ACE inhibitors/ARBs are the cornerstone. SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) have proven nephroprotective effect independent of glucose lowering (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY trials) — now standard of care for proteinuric CKD. Diet: low-protein diet (0.6-0.8 g/kg/day) in pre-dialysis CKD may slow progression. Low potassium diet in hyperkalaemia. Low phosphate diet + phosphate binders (calcium carbonate, sevelamer) for hyperphosphataemia. Low sodium (<2g/day) for BP control. Anaemia management: iron stores first (oral or IV iron). Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs — erythropoietin, darbepoetin) if Hb <10 g/dL after iron replete. Haemoglobin target 10-11.5 g/dL (higher targets associated with cardiovascular risk). 7. Renal Replacement Therapy Haemodialysis (HD): blood is filtered through an artificial kidney (dialyser) 3 times per week, 4-5 hours per session, typically at a dialysis centre. Vascular access: arteriovenous fistula (preferred — lower infection rate), AV graft, or central venous catheter (temporary). Peritoneal dialysis (PD): continuous ambulatory PD (CAPD) or automated PD (APD) using the peritoneum as the dialysis membrane. Home-based, more physiological, preserves residual renal

ambulatory PD (CAPD) or automated PD (APD) using the peritoneum as the dialysis membrane. Home-based, more physiological, preserves residual renal function longer. Preferred in children, young patients, remote areas, diabetes (better volume control). Kidney transplantation: the optimal treatment for eligible ESRD patients. Best quality of life and survival. Live donor (family member) preferred. Deceased donor (cadaveric) transplant program growing in India. Requires lifelong immunosuppression (tacrolimus + mycophenolate + prednisolone). In India, ESRD care is mainly funded out-of-pocket. Pradhan Mantri National Dialysis Programme provides free dialysis at government hospitals. Organ donation rates in India remain very low (~0.8 pmp vs 20-30 pmp in Spain/USA), limiting deceased donor

free dialysis at government hospitals. Organ donation rates in India remain very low (~0.8 pmp vs 20-30 pmp in Spain/USA), limiting deceased donor transplants. 8. Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) AKI is defined as: rise in serum creatinine ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours, or ≥1.5x baseline within 7 days, or urine output <0.5 ml/kg/hr for ≥6 hours. Causes: Pre-renal (60-70%): hypovolaemia, sepsis, cardiac failure, hepatic failure, NSAIDs/ACEi in low flow states — all reduce renal perfusion. Intrinsic renal: acute tubular necrosis (ATN) — ischaemia, nephrotoxins (gentamicin, contrast, myoglobin), glomerulonephritis, vasculitis. Post-renal (obstructive): stones, prostatic hypertrophy, tumour. Management: treat underlying cause. Fluid resuscitation for pre-renal AKI. Avoid nephrotoxins. Monitor

stones, prostatic hypertrophy, tumour. Management: treat underlying cause. Fluid resuscitation for pre-renal AKI. Avoid nephrotoxins. Monitor potassium, bicarbonate, fluid balance. Indications for emergency dialysis (AEIOU): Acidosis severe (pH <7.1), Electrolyte (K+ >6.5 mmol/L refractory), Intoxication (methanol, ethylene glycol, lithium), Overload (refractory pulmonary oedema), Uraemia (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding). AKI in pregnancy: septic abortion, eclampsia, HELLP syndrome, acute fatty liver of pregnancy. Cortical necrosis unique to obstetric AKI. Obstructive uropathy from gravid uterus. Fetal outcomes depend on gestational age and aetiology. 9. Nephrotic and Nephritic Syndromes Nephrotic syndrome: heavy proteinuria (>3.5g/day), hypoalbuminaemia (<3g/dL), oedema,

age and aetiology. 9. Nephrotic and Nephritic Syndromes Nephrotic syndrome: heavy proteinuria (>3.5g/day), hypoalbuminaemia (<3g/dL), oedema, hyperlipidaemia, lipiduria. Causes: minimal change disease (most common in children — responds well to steroids), FSGS (most common adult primary nephrotic syndrome in India), membranous nephropathy, diabetic nephropathy. Nephritic syndrome: haematuria (red cell casts in urine), proteinuria, hypertension, oliguria, oedema. Causes: post-streptococcal GN (common in children after throat/skin streptococcal infection), IgA nephropathy, lupus nephritis, ANCA vasculitis. Investigations: 24-hour urine protein or UACR, urine microscopy, serum complement (C3, C4 — low in post-strep GN and lupus), ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM, ASO titre, renal biopsy (definitive

or UACR, urine microscopy, serum complement (C3, C4 — low in post-strep GN and lupus), ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM, ASO titre, renal biopsy (definitive diagnosis). 10. Kidney Disease Prevention in India Primary prevention: control diabetes and hypertension aggressively. Avoid nephrotoxic medications (NSAIDs, certain antibiotics). Adequate hydration in hot weather and physical work. Avoid traditional herbal remedies with heavy metals (lead, mercury, arsenic) — common cause of CKD in India. Secondary prevention: screen all diabetics and hypertensives annually for CKD with eGFR and UACR. Treat microalbuminuria with ACE inhibitors/ARBs immediately. Early referral to nephrology at GFR <30 or rapid decline. India-specific: CKDu (CKD of unknown aetiology) in farming communities of Andhra Pradesh,

Early referral to nephrology at GFR <30 or rapid decline. India-specific: CKDu (CKD of unknown aetiology) in farming communities of Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Sri Lanka: dehydration, heat stress, agrochemical exposure. Prevention requires occupational measures (adequate hydration, shade, protective equipment) and community screening. National Organ and Tissue Transplant Organisation (NOTTO) coordinates deceased donor organ sharing. Initiatives to increase deceased donor rates: mandatory discussion of organ donation at ICU admissions, ICD coding for brain death, sensitization of medical professionals and public.

Liver Disease — Hepatitis, Cirrhosis and NAFLD 1. Overview The liver is the body's largest internal organ, performing over 500 metabolic functions including protein synthesis, gluconeogenesis, lipid metabolism, bile production, detoxification, and immune function. Liver disease encompasses a spectrum from mild elevation of liver enzymes to liver failure and cancer. In India, liver disease is a significant public health problem. Viral hepatitis (B and C) accounts for the largest share of liver disease burden globally. Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), now reclassified as Metabolic Associated Fatty Liver Disease (MAFLD), is the fastest growing liver disease, driven by obesity, diabetes, and the metabolic syndrome. 2. Viral Hepatitis Hepatitis A and E: faeco-oral transmission

fastest growing liver disease, driven by obesity, diabetes, and the metabolic syndrome. 2. Viral Hepatitis Hepatitis A and E: faeco-oral transmission (contaminated water, food). Self-limiting acute hepatitis. Hepatitis A: worldwide, children in endemic areas. Hepatitis E: major cause of acute hepatitis in India, particularly during floods/monsoon. Very high mortality in pregnancy (15-25% — fulminant liver failure). No specific treatment. Hepatitis B (HBV): blood-borne (IV drug use, needlestick, unprotected sex) and vertical (mother to child at delivery). 300 million chronically infected worldwide, 40+ million in India (3.7% prevalence). Leads to chronic hepatitis, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). HBsAg is the surface antigen used for diagnosis; HBeAg indicates high

Leads to chronic hepatitis, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). HBsAg is the surface antigen used for diagnosis; HBeAg indicates high infectivity; HBV DNA quantifies viral load. Treatment of chronic HBV: tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF) or entecavir (ETV) — preferred first-line oral antivirals. Suppress viral replication, reduce risk of cirrhosis and HCC. Not curative — long-term or lifelong therapy often required. Pegylated interferon-alpha (Peg-IFN) is an alternative finite duration treatment. Hepatitis C (HCV): blood-borne. 58 million chronically infected globally. India: 6-12 million estimated. Major cause of cirrhosis and HCC. HCV is CURABLE with Direct-Acting Antivirals (DAAs): sofosbuvir-velpatasvir, sofosbuvir-ledipasvir, glecaprevir-pibrentasvir. 8-12 week

and HCC. HCV is CURABLE with Direct-Acting Antivirals (DAAs): sofosbuvir-velpatasvir, sofosbuvir-ledipasvir, glecaprevir-pibrentasvir. 8-12 week treatment, cure rates >95%. Cost has fallen dramatically — affordable generics available in India. 3. Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease (NAFLD/MAFLD) NAFLD is defined as >5% hepatic steatosis (fat in liver) without significant alcohol use. It is the most common liver disease globally, affecting approximately 25% of the world population. In India, NAFLD affects ~25-35% of the urban population, closely linked to the epidemic of metabolic syndrome. Spectrum: Simple steatosis (NAFL) → Non-Alcoholic Steatohepatitis (NASH — steatosis + inflammation + injury) → Fibrosis → Cirrhosis → HCC. Approximately 20% of NAFLD progresses to NASH; 20-30% of NASH

(NASH — steatosis + inflammation + injury) → Fibrosis → Cirrhosis → HCC. Approximately 20% of NAFLD progresses to NASH; 20-30% of NASH progresses to cirrhosis over 10 years. Diagnosis: Liver ultrasound (first-line screening). FibroScan (transient elastography) measures liver stiffness (fibrosis). NAFLD Fibrosis Score (NFS) or FIB-4 index (non-invasive scoring using routine bloods). Liver biopsy — gold standard for staging fibrosis and confirming NASH. Treatment: no approved medications for NAFLD/NASH yet (as of 2024). Cornerstone: weight loss (7-10% body weight reduces steatosis, >10% improves fibrosis). Diet: Mediterranean diet. Exercise: both aerobic and resistance. Control metabolic risk factors. SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide) show promising results in

both aerobic and resistance. Control metabolic risk factors. SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide) show promising results in trials. Resmetirom (THR-beta agonist) FDA approved 2024 for non-cirrhotic NASH with fibrosis. 4. Alcoholic Liver Disease Alcoholic Liver Disease (ALD) encompasses fatty liver (reversible), alcoholic hepatitis (potentially severe and life-threatening), and alcoholic cirrhosis. Heavy drinking (>4 standard drinks/day for men, >2 for women) over years is required. Alcoholic hepatitis: acute presentation with jaundice, fever, hepatomegaly, tender liver, leukocytosis. Discriminant Function (Maddrey) ≥32 or MELD >20 = severe — prednisolone 40 mg/day x 28 days reduces short-term mortality. Pentoxifylline is an alternative. Liver transplant for

≥32 or MELD >20 = severe — prednisolone 40 mg/day x 28 days reduces short-term mortality. Pentoxifylline is an alternative. Liver transplant for selected cases not responding to steroids (excellent outcomes). Abstinence is the most important intervention at all stages of ALD. Even established cirrhosis can compensate and 5-year survival improves dramatically with complete abstinence. 5. Cirrhosis Cirrhosis is the end-stage of chronic liver disease — extensive fibrosis distorting normal liver architecture with formation of regenerative nodules. Leads to impaired liver function and portal hypertension. Compensated vs Decompensated: compensated cirrhosis — no major complications, may be asymptomatic. Decompensated — ascites, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP), variceal bleeding, hepatic

cirrhosis — no major complications, may be asymptomatic. Decompensated — ascites, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP), variceal bleeding, hepatic encephalopathy, hepatorenal syndrome (HRS). Ascites: most common complication. Na restriction (<2g/day) + spironolactone (100-400 mg) + furosemide (40-160 mg). Therapeutic paracentesis for large-volume ascites. TIPS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) for refractory ascites. Avoid NSAIDs and nephrotoxins. Hepatic encephalopathy: altered consciousness due to uraemic toxins (ammonia) reaching brain. Lactulose (titrated to 2-3 soft stools/day) is first-line. Rifaximin (non-absorbable antibiotic) prevents recurrence. Identify and treat precipitants: GI bleed, infection, constipation, dehydration, sedatives. Variceal bleeding: medical

prevents recurrence. Identify and treat precipitants: GI bleed, infection, constipation, dehydration, sedatives. Variceal bleeding: medical emergency — IV terlipressin/octreotide, broad-spectrum antibiotics (prophylactic), endoscopic variceal band ligation (EVL) within 12 hours. Non-selective beta-blockers (propranolol, carvedilol) for primary prevention of variceal bleeding. 6. Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC) HCC is the most common primary liver cancer and the third leading cause of cancer death globally. Approximately 80% arise in cirrhotic livers. Major risk factors: HBV, HCV, alcoholic cirrhosis, NASH cirrhosis, aflatoxin B1 exposure (food contamination — maize, groundnuts in India). Surveillance: all cirrhotic patients and HBV carriers (without cirrhosis but with risk factors) should

contamination — maize, groundnuts in India). Surveillance: all cirrhotic patients and HBV carriers (without cirrhosis but with risk factors) should undergo 6-monthly liver ultrasound ± AFP for HCC surveillance. Staging: Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer (BCLC) system. Treatment: curative options (resection, liver transplant, radiofrequency ablation/RFA) for early-stage. TACE (transarterial chemoembolization) for intermediate. Sorafenib, lenvatinib (targeted therapy), atezolizumab-bevacizumab (immunotherapy combination) for advanced. 7. Acute Liver Failure Acute liver failure (ALF): rapid deterioration of liver function in a patient without pre-existing liver disease — coagulopathy (INR >1.5) + encephalopathy within 26 weeks of illness onset. Causes in India: HAV (most common in children), HEV

liver disease — coagulopathy (INR >1.5) + encephalopathy within 26 weeks of illness onset. Causes in India: HAV (most common in children), HEV (most common in adults, especially pregnancy), HBV, drugs (antitubercular drugs, paracetamol overdose, herbal remedies with hepatotoxins). Management: ICU care. Lactulose + rifaximin for encephalopathy. Vitamin K, fresh frozen plasma for coagulopathy (if bleeding). Paracetamol-induced ALF: N-acetylcysteine IV (highly effective if given within 8-16 hours). Monitor for cerebral oedema, renal failure, hypoglycaemia, infections. Emergency liver transplant: the only definitive treatment for ALF meeting King's College Criteria. Transplant within 72 hours dramatically improves survival. Extremely limited availability in India. 8. Hepatitis B Vaccination

Hepatitis B Vaccination and Prevention Hepatitis B vaccine is one of the most important vaccines. The universal childhood immunisation schedule in India includes HBV vaccine at birth (within 24 hours for MTCT prevention), 6 weeks, 10 weeks, and 14 weeks. Catch-up vaccination: all unvaccinated adolescents and adults at risk (healthcare workers, sexual contacts of HBsAg-positive individuals, IDUs, dialysis patients). Three doses (0, 1, 6 months) or accelerated schedule. Prevention of hepatitis C: no vaccine available. Harm reduction for IDUs (needle exchange, OST). Screen blood donors. Safe sex practices. Universal precautions in healthcare settings. Screen all PLHIV and

for IDUs (needle exchange, OST). Screen blood donors. Safe sex practices. Universal precautions in healthcare settings. Screen all PLHIV and high-risk individuals for HCV. Aflatoxin prevention: proper grain storage (dry, ventilated), regulatory testing of food commodities, avoiding consumption of visibly mouldy food — important HCC prevention measure in India. 9. Drug-Induced Liver Injury (DILI) DILI is the most common cause of acute liver failure in developed countries and a significant problem in India. Antitubercular drugs (isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide) are the most common cause of DILI in India — occurring in 5-28% of patients on standard RIPE therapy. Other important causes in India: herbal and traditional medicines (Ayurvedic preparations containing heavy metals, herbs with

standard RIPE therapy. Other important causes in India: herbal and traditional medicines (Ayurvedic preparations containing heavy metals, herbs with pyrrolizidine alkaloids), antiretrovirals (nevirapine, efavirenz), complementary and alternative medicines, dietary supplements. Monitoring: baseline LFTs before starting ATT. Repeat at 2 and 4 weeks, then monthly. Stop ATT if ALT >5x ULN or ALT >3x ULN with symptoms/jaundice. Introduce drugs sequentially when reintroducing after DILI resolution. 10. Liver Health in India — Prevention Hepatitis B: universal infant vaccination with zero dose at birth within 24 hours is the most effective intervention against HBV-related cirrhosis and HCC. Safe injection practices and blood safety. Hepatitis C: India is scaling up DAA-based treatment through

against HBV-related cirrhosis and HCC. Safe injection practices and blood safety. Hepatitis C: India is scaling up DAA-based treatment through the National Viral Hepatitis Control Programme (NVHCP) — targets treating all HCV-infected individuals. Free Sofosbuvir + Daclatasvir provided at government hospitals. NAFLD prevention: population-level interventions against metabolic syndrome — reducing consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages, promoting physical activity, reformulation of processed food, school-based nutrition programmes. Alcohol: strengthening alcohol policy — taxation, restricting advertising, improving access to treatment for AUD, support for community-based de-addiction programmes. Food safety: enforcement of mycotoxin (aflatoxin) limits in food commodities, particularly

AUD, support for community-based de-addiction programmes. Food safety: enforcement of mycotoxin (aflatoxin) limits in food commodities, particularly groundnuts and maize. Awareness about safe food storage practices in homes and small businesses.

Malaria — Diagnosis, Treatment and Prevention 1. Epidemiology and Global Burden Malaria remains a major global public health problem, with the WHO World Malaria Report 2022 reporting 247 million cases and 619,000 deaths globally in 2021. Sub-Saharan Africa bears 95% of the global burden, but South Asia — particularly India — contributes significantly. India accounts for approximately 83% of malaria cases in the WHO South-East Asia Region. Odisha, Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, and North-Eastern states have the highest transmission. India contributes substantially to Plasmodium vivax burden — the globally neglected second-most-common malaria species. India achieved WHO certification of malaria elimination in several states (e.g., Sri Lanka in the region eliminated malaria in

malaria species. India achieved WHO certification of malaria elimination in several states (e.g., Sri Lanka in the region eliminated malaria in 2016). India's National Strategic Plan for Malaria Elimination aims to eliminate malaria from 21 states and 4 union territories by 2022, and from the entire country by 2030. 2. Parasitology and Life Cycle Five Plasmodium species infect humans: P. falciparum (most dangerous, predominates in Africa and coastal India), P. vivax (most widespread, predominates in India and Asia — produces dormant liver stage), P. malariae (causes quartan malaria, persists decades), P. ovale (rare, West Africa), P. knowlesi (zoonotic, Southeast Asia — can cause severe malaria). Life cycle: female Anopheles mosquito injects sporozoites during blood meal → sporozoites

(zoonotic, Southeast Asia — can cause severe malaria). Life cycle: female Anopheles mosquito injects sporozoites during blood meal → sporozoites migrate to liver (hepatic stage, no symptoms) → multiply into thousands of merozoites → merozoites released into bloodstream → infect red blood cells (RBCs) → asexual multiplication (ring → trophozoite → schizont → merozoite rupture) → RBC rupture causes fever cycle. P. vivax and P. ovale produce hypnozoites — dormant liver forms that can reactivate months to years after the primary infection, causing relapsing malaria. Only primaquine/tafenoquine can eliminate hypnozoites (radical cure). The sexual stage (gametocytes) circulates in the blood and is taken up by mosquitoes during feeding, completing the transmission cycle. P. falciparum

The sexual stage (gametocytes) circulates in the blood and is taken up by mosquitoes during feeding, completing the transmission cycle. P. falciparum gametocytes persist for weeks even after treatment, maintaining transmission potential. 3. Clinical Features Classic malaria paroxysm: cold stage (rigors, chills, severe shaking, 1-2 hours) → hot stage (high fever 39-41°C, headache, vomiting, 2-6 hours) → sweating stage (drenching sweats, fever breaks, exhaustion). Cycle repeats every 48 hours (P. vivax, P. falciparum) or 72 hours (P. malariae). Other symptoms: headache, myalgia, arthralgia, anorexia, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, splenomegaly (enlarged spleen — chronic malaria), anaemia (pallor, fatigue, dyspnea) due to destruction of infected and uninfected RBCs. Uncomplicated malaria:

(enlarged spleen — chronic malaria), anaemia (pallor, fatigue, dyspnea) due to destruction of infected and uninfected RBCs. Uncomplicated malaria: above symptoms without evidence of end-organ dysfunction. Severe malaria (WHO criteria): cerebral malaria (impaired consciousness, seizures — most important criterion), severe anaemia (Hb <7 g/dL in adults), acute kidney injury, pulmonary oedema/ARDS, spontaneous bleeding, hypoglycemia, metabolic acidosis, haemoglobinuria (blackwater fever), hyperparasitaemia (>5% infected RBCs). Cerebral malaria: diffuse encephalopathy — symmetrical, no focal signs. Coma (Blantyre/Glasgow Coma Scale). Seizures (especially children). Retinal haemorrhages and papilloedema on fundoscopy. Poor prognostic sign: abnormal posturing (decerebrate/decorticate), absence

children). Retinal haemorrhages and papilloedema on fundoscopy. Poor prognostic sign: abnormal posturing (decerebrate/decorticate), absence of corneal reflex. 4.
