Universal vaccination: Pentavalent vaccine (DTP-HB-Hib) and PCV (Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccine) are part of India's Universal Immunisation Programme and reduce bacterial respiratory infections significantly in children.

Stroke (Brain Attack) — Comprehensive Clinical Guide 1. Definition and Epidemiology Stroke is defined as a sudden neurological deficit attributable to focal brain, spinal cord, or retinal ischemia or haemorrhage, with imaging or pathological evidence. A Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA) has the same mechanism but symptoms resolve within 24 hours (usually within 1 hour) without infarction on diffusion MRI. Stroke is the second leading cause of death globally and the leading cause of adult disability. In India, the incidence of stroke is 105-152/100,000/year, with approximately 1.8 million new strokes occurring annually. India is witnessing increasing stroke burden in younger adults due to hypertension, diabetes, and atrial fibrillation. Time is Brain: every minute a large vessel occlusion

stroke burden in younger adults due to hypertension, diabetes, and atrial fibrillation. Time is Brain: every minute a large vessel occlusion stroke goes untreated, approximately 1.9 million neurons, 14 billion synapses, and 12 km of axons are destroyed. Rapid recognition and treatment is the single most important determinant of outcome. 2. Classification and Mechanisms Ischemic stroke (87% of all strokes): caused by focal brain ischemia due to vascular occlusion. TOAST Classification: Large artery atherothrombosis (carotid/vertebrobasilar), Cardioembolic (atrial fibrillation, valvular disease, intracardiac thrombus), Small vessel lacunar (lipohyalinosis of perforating arteries in hypertension), Other determined cause, Undetermined (cryptogenic). Haemorrhagic stroke (13%): Intracerebral

of perforating arteries in hypertension), Other determined cause, Undetermined (cryptogenic). Haemorrhagic stroke (13%): Intracerebral haemorrhage (ICH, 10%) — spontaneous bleeding into brain parenchyma, most commonly from rupture of small penetrating arteries damaged by chronic hypertension. Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH, 3%) — bleeding into subarachnoid space, usually from ruptured intracranial aneurysm or AVM. Large vessel occlusion (LVO): occlusion of proximal cerebral arteries (ICA, M1/M2 MCA, basilar artery). Responsible for 20-25% of ischemic strokes but ~50% of stroke disability. Clinically characterized by devastating deficits and amenable to mechanical thrombectomy. 3. Stroke Recognition — FAST and Beyond FAST: Face drooping (unilateral facial droop, asymmetric smile), Arm

and amenable to mechanical thrombectomy. 3. Stroke Recognition — FAST and Beyond FAST: Face drooping (unilateral facial droop, asymmetric smile), Arm weakness (one arm drifts down when both raised), Speech disturbance (slurred, garbled, or unable to speak/understand), Time to call 112/911. BE-FAST (more sensitive for posterior circulation stroke): Balance loss (sudden), Eyes (sudden vision change in one or both), Face, Arms, Speech, Time. Other stroke symptoms: sudden severe headache 'worst headache of life' (SAH — thunderclap headache), sudden loss of vision in one eye (amaurosis fugax — retinal artery occlusion), sudden vertigo with ataxia (posterior fossa ischemia), confusion, memory loss. Stroke mimics: hypoglycemia (most important — always check blood sugar), Todd's palsy

with ataxia (posterior fossa ischemia), confusion, memory loss. Stroke mimics: hypoglycemia (most important — always check blood sugar), Todd's palsy (post-seizure weakness), complex migraine, hypertensive encephalopathy, brain tumour, functional neurological disorder, Wernicke's encephalopathy. 4. Pre-Hospital and Emergency Management CALL 112/911 immediately. Do NOT drive patient to hospital. Ambulance is critical for pre-notification and direct access to stroke team. In-ambulance assessment: FAST, blood glucose, time of symptom onset, airway-breathing-circulation, IV access, ECG. Pre-notify stroke centre: 'STEMI for the brain' — activate Stroke Code. CT brain (non-contrast) within 25 minutes of arrival: rules out haemorrhage (contraindication to thrombolysis), detects early ischemic

Stroke Code. CT brain (non-contrast) within 25 minutes of arrival: rules out haemorrhage (contraindication to thrombolysis), detects early ischemic changes (ASPECTS score). CT angiography: detects LVO for thrombectomy consideration. CT perfusion: identifies salvageable penumbra. IV thrombolysis with alteplase (tPA): 0.9 mg/kg IV (max 90 mg, 10% as bolus, rest over 60 min) within 4.5 hours of symptom onset for eligible ischemic stroke patients. Not for haemorrhagic stroke, recent surgery, anticoagulation, severe hypertension (>185/110 — must be controlled first), or recent stroke. 5. Mechanical Thrombectomy Mechanical thrombectomy (MT) is the most significant advancement in stroke treatment of the 21st century. Stent-retriever and aspiration catheters physically remove clots from proximal

most significant advancement in stroke treatment of the 21st century. Stent-retriever and aspiration catheters physically remove clots from proximal cerebral vessels under fluoroscopic guidance. Indication: LVO ischemic stroke, within 6-24 hours of last known well, with salvageable tissue on imaging (DAWN, DEFUSE-3 criteria). mTICI 2b-3 reperfusion achieved in >80% of cases at experienced centres. Outcomes: MT increases the proportion achieving functional independence (mRS 0-2) by ~25% vs medical treatment alone. A number needed to treat (NNT) of ~2.6 makes MT one of the most effective interventions in medicine. In India, MT capability is concentrated in major urban centres (AIIMS, CMC Vellore, Apollo, Fortis, PGI Chandigarh). Expanding to tier-2 cities is a priority. Telestroke

is concentrated in major urban centres (AIIMS, CMC Vellore, Apollo, Fortis, PGI Chandigarh). Expanding to tier-2 cities is a priority. Telestroke programmes can extend expert evaluation to district hospitals. 6. Secondary Prevention of Stroke Antiplatelets: aspirin 75-150 mg daily + clopidogrel 75 mg for 21 days (high-risk TIA and minor stroke — POINT/CHANCE trial), then aspirin alone. Ticagrelor + aspirin being evaluated. For cardioembolic stroke — anticoagulation, not antiplatelet. Atrial fibrillation and stroke: AF is the most important modifiable risk factor for cardioembolic stroke. CHA2DS2-VASc score ≥2 in men, ≥3 in women → oral anticoagulation (NOAC preferred over warfarin: apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran — lower bleeding risk). Blood pressure management: target <130/80 mmHg.

(NOAC preferred over warfarin: apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran — lower bleeding risk). Blood pressure management: target <130/80 mmHg. Start antihypertensive treatment 24-48 hours after stroke in stable patients. ACE inhibitor + diuretic (perindopril + indapamide, PROGRESS trial) reduces recurrence by 43%. Statin therapy: high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40-80 mg) regardless of baseline cholesterol for all ischemic stroke/TIA patients. Reduces stroke recurrence and coronary events. Carotid artery stenosis: symptomatic carotid stenosis >50% → carotid endarterectomy (CEA) within 2 weeks (best within 48 hours for high-risk TIA). CAS (carotid artery stenting) for high surgical risk patients. 7. Haemorrhagic Stroke Management Intracerebral Haemorrhage (ICH): acute BP lowering to SBP <140

artery stenting) for high surgical risk patients. 7. Haemorrhagic Stroke Management Intracerebral Haemorrhage (ICH): acute BP lowering to SBP <140 mmHg is recommended (INTERACT2, ATACH-2 partial). Reverse anticoagulation: vitamin K + FFP/PCC for warfarin; idarucizumab for dabigatran; andexanet alfa for Xa inhibitors. Surgical indications for ICH: cerebellar haematoma >3 cm or causing brainstem compression (urgent surgery); lobar ICH with deterioration. Basal ganglia and thalamic haematomas — no surgical benefit in most cases. Minimally invasive surgery (MIS) for ICH under trial. Subarachnoid Haemorrhage (SAH): immediate CT (100% sensitive in 12 hours of ictus), then lumbar puncture if CT negative but thunderclap headache strong clinically. Secure aneurysm: coiling (endovascular) or

in 12 hours of ictus), then lumbar puncture if CT negative but thunderclap headache strong clinically. Secure aneurysm: coiling (endovascular) or clipping (surgical) urgently. IV nimodipine 60 mg every 4 hours for 21 days to prevent vasospasm. 8. Stroke Rehabilitation Rehabilitation begins within 24-48 hours of stroke stabilization. Early mobilization reduces pneumonia, DVT, pressure sores, and depression. Stroke unit care (vs general ward) reduces death and dependency by ~25%. Multidisciplinary stroke team: physiotherapy (motor recovery, gait training, balance), occupational therapy (ADL retraining, hand function, adaptive equipment), speech-language therapy (dysphasia, aphasia, dysphagia), neuropsychology (cognitive rehabilitation, depression, anxiety). Principles of

equipment), speech-language therapy (dysphasia, aphasia, dysphagia), neuropsychology (cognitive rehabilitation, depression, anxiety). Principles of neuroplasticity-based rehabilitation: task-specific repetitive practice drives cortical reorganization. Constraint-induced movement therapy (CIMT), robot-assisted therapy, virtual reality, non-invasive brain stimulation (rTMS, tDCS) are emerging modalities. Aphasia after stroke: 30% of stroke patients. Language rehabilitation (speech therapy) should be intensive and prolonged. Communication aids, family education, aphasia groups, and community reintegration support. 9. TIA and Stroke Prevention TIA is a medical emergency. 10-15% risk of early stroke after TIA — ABCD2 score (Age, BP, Clinical features, Duration, Diabetes) helps risk-stratify.

TIA is a medical emergency. 10-15% risk of early stroke after TIA — ABCD2 score (Age, BP, Clinical features, Duration, Diabetes) helps risk-stratify. High-risk TIA (ABCD2 ≥4 or symptomatic carotid stenosis or AF) → immediate hospitalization, urgent evaluation, and treatment within 24 hours. Brain and vascular imaging: DWI-MRI is more sensitive than CT for acute ischemia. MRA or CTA to assess intracranial vessels. Carotid ultrasound and Doppler for carotid stenosis. Cardiac investigations: ECG (AF), 24-hour Holter, echocardiogram (thrombus, valvular disease, patent foramen ovale). Long-term risk factor control: The single most preventable risk factor for stroke in India is uncontrolled hypertension. Atrial fibrillation must be detected and anticoagulated. Diabetes control reduces

risk factor for stroke in India is uncontrolled hypertension. Atrial fibrillation must be detected and anticoagulated. Diabetes control reduces microvascular risk (lacunar strokes). Smoking cessation reduces stroke risk by 50% within 2 years. 10. Stroke Systems of Care in India India's stroke care faces major challenges: most acute strokes arrive beyond the thrombolysis window; limited stroke unit availability; low awareness of FAST symptoms; inadequate ambulance infrastructure; high out-of-pocket treatment costs. Stroke units: shown to reduce mortality and dependency. National AIIMS-based stroke registry and programmes like INSPIRE (Indian Stroke Programme Initiative for Research and Education) are building capacity. State-based stroke networks (e.g., Tamil Nadu) are improving access.

Stroke Programme Initiative for Research and Education) are building capacity. State-based stroke networks (e.g., Tamil Nadu) are improving access. Telestroke: remote stroke neurologist consultation via video call enables 24/7 expert support for district hospitals with basic CT capability. A hub-and-spoke model is the most scalable approach for India. Post-stroke care: only ~20% of Indian stroke patients access formal rehabilitation. Community-based rehabilitation (CBR), family training by OT and PT, and low-cost orthotic devices are critical for the majority who cannot access urban tertiary rehab centres.

Thyroid Disorders — Hypothyroidism, Hyperthyroidism and Cancer 1. Thyroid Physiology The thyroid gland is a butterfly-shaped gland in the anterior neck that produces thyroid hormones T4 (thyroxine) and T3 (triiodothyronine). These regulate metabolism, growth, cardiac function, thermogenesis, and development of the nervous system. The hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid (HPT) axis regulates thyroid hormone levels. TRH (thyrotropin-releasing hormone) from hypothalamus stimulates TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone) from pituitary → TSH stimulates T4/T3 production. T4/T3 exert negative feedback on both hypothalamus and pituitary. T4 is the major secreted hormone and is converted to active T3 (3-5x more potent) in peripheral tissues by deiodinases. Iodine is essential for thyroid hormone synthesis —

hormone and is converted to active T3 (3-5x more potent) in peripheral tissues by deiodinases. Iodine is essential for thyroid hormone synthesis — iodine deficiency is a major public health issue in India. 2. Hypothyroidism Hypothyroidism is deficiency of thyroid hormone. Primary hypothyroidism (thyroid gland disease) is most common — elevated TSH, low free T4. Secondary is rare (pituitary failure — low TSH and low T4). Subclinical hypothyroidism: elevated TSH with normal T4. Causes: Hashimoto's thyroiditis (autoimmune — most common in adults, positive anti-TPO antibodies), iodine deficiency, post-radioiodine/post-thyroidectomy, drugs (lithium, amiodarone, interferon), congenital. Symptoms: fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin and hair, hair loss, bradycardia,

amiodarone, interferon), congenital. Symptoms: fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin and hair, hair loss, bradycardia, hoarse voice, myalgia, muscle weakness, depression, memory impairment, menstrual irregularity, infertility, goitre. Severe/myxoedema coma: rare life-threatening emergency — extreme hypothyroidism presenting as stupor/coma, hypothermia, hypoventilation, hyponatraemia, bradycardia, hypoglycaemia. Triggers: cold exposure, infection, sedatives. ICU management with IV levothyroxine, hydrocortisone, warming, airway management. 3. Hypothyroidism Treatment Levothyroxine (LT4): the treatment of choice. Pure synthetic T4 taken orally once daily, 30-60 minutes before breakfast (absorption reduced by coffee, calcium, iron). Starting dose: 1.6 mcg/kg/day (full

T4 taken orally once daily, 30-60 minutes before breakfast (absorption reduced by coffee, calcium, iron). Starting dose: 1.6 mcg/kg/day (full replacement) OR start low (25-50 mcg) in elderly/cardiac patients. TSH monitoring: recheck TSH 4-6 weeks after initiating or changing dose. Target TSH: 0.5-2.5 mIU/L for most adults. Slightly higher target (1-4 mIU/L) acceptable for elderly or those with coronary artery disease. Subclinical hypothyroidism: treat if TSH >10 mIU/L, or TSH 4.5-10 with symptoms, pregnancy, or positive anti-TPO antibodies. Some require no treatment — monitor 6-monthly. Hypothyroidism in pregnancy: critical — foetal brain development depends on maternal thyroid hormones in first trimester. TSH targets: <2.5 mIU/L in first trimester. Screen all pregnant women.

brain development depends on maternal thyroid hormones in first trimester. TSH targets: <2.5 mIU/L in first trimester. Screen all pregnant women. Levothyroxine dose typically needs to increase by 25-30% in pregnancy. 4. Hyperthyroidism Hyperthyroidism: excess thyroid hormone. Thyrotoxicosis: the clinical syndrome resulting from excess thyroid hormones (any cause, including exogenous). Low TSH with elevated free T4/T3 on blood tests. Graves' disease: autoimmune — TSH receptor-stimulating antibodies (TRAb). Most common cause of hyperthyroidism (60-80%). Classic triad: diffuse goitre, exophthalmos (proptosis — eye disease due to orbital inflammation), pretibial myxoedema (dermopathy — rare). Thyroid bruit may be present. Toxic multinodular goitre: common in elderly in iodine-deficient areas

pretibial myxoedema (dermopathy — rare). Thyroid bruit may be present. Toxic multinodular goitre: common in elderly in iodine-deficient areas (India, mountain regions). Toxic adenoma: single autonomous hot nodule. Thyroiditis (subacute, postpartum, drug-induced — amiodarone): release of pre-formed T4/T3; initially hyperthyroid then may become hypothyroid. Symptoms: weight loss despite increased appetite, heat intolerance, sweating, palpitations (tachycardia, atrial fibrillation), tremor, anxiety, irritability, diarrhoea, oligomenorrhoea, muscle weakness, goitre, exophthalmos (Graves'). Apathetic hyperthyroidism in elderly: weight loss, heart failure, depression. 5. Hyperthyroidism Treatment Anti-thyroid drugs: carbimazole (20-60 mg/day) or methimazole (15-60 mg/day) — block thyroid

heart failure, depression. 5. Hyperthyroidism Treatment Anti-thyroid drugs: carbimazole (20-60 mg/day) or methimazole (15-60 mg/day) — block thyroid peroxidase enzyme. Propylthiouracil (PTU) — also blocks T4 to T3 conversion; preferred in first trimester of pregnancy. Side effects: agranulocytosis (rare but serious — check FBC if fever/sore throat), hepatotoxicity. Beta-blockers (propranolol 40-120 mg/day or atenolol): control symptoms (palpitations, tremor, anxiety) while awaiting euthyroidism from anti-thyroid drugs. Do not treat the thyroid itself. Radioiodine (RAI, I-131): ablates thyroid tissue. Curative in 80-90% with one dose. Standard treatment in UK/USA for Graves'. Results in permanent hypothyroidism (usually within 6-18 months). Contraindicated in pregnancy, active

Standard treatment in UK/USA for Graves'. Results in permanent hypothyroidism (usually within 6-18 months). Contraindicated in pregnancy, active moderate-severe orbitopathy. Avoid close contact with children/pregnant women for 1-2 weeks post-treatment. Thyroidectomy: indicated for large goitres causing compressive symptoms (dysphagia, stridor), suspicious nodule, Graves' with large goitre or orbitopathy, or patient preference. Total thyroidectomy preferred. Complications: hypoparathyroidism, recurrent laryngeal nerve injury. 6. Thyroid Storm (Thyrotoxic Crisis) Thyroid storm is a rare, life-threatening exacerbation of hyperthyroidism. Precipitants: surgery, infection, trauma, contrast media, acute illness in undertreated hyperthyroid patient. Mortality 10-30% even with treatment. Clinical

surgery, infection, trauma, contrast media, acute illness in undertreated hyperthyroid patient. Mortality 10-30% even with treatment. Clinical features: extreme hyperthermia (>40°C), tachycardia (often AF), hypertension followed by haemodynamic instability, agitation, confusion, seizures, vomiting, diarrhoea, jaundice. Treatment (Burch-Wartofsky Point Scale guides diagnosis): PTU 500-1000 mg loading THEN 250 mg every 4 hours (blocks new synthesis AND T4→T3 conversion) OR high-dose methimazole. 1-2 hours after PTU: Lugol's iodine (SSKI) to block thyroid hormone release. IV hydrocortisone (stress doses). Propranolol IV. Treat precipitant. Active cooling. ICU admission. 7. Thyroid Nodules and Cancer Thyroid nodules are common — palpable in 5-7% of adults, detected on ultrasound in 20-76%.

cooling. ICU admission. 7. Thyroid Nodules and Cancer Thyroid nodules are common — palpable in 5-7% of adults, detected on ultrasound in 20-76%. The vast majority (>95%) are benign. Key concern: identify the malignant 5%. Evaluation: thyroid ultrasound — assess nodule characteristics (size, echogenicity, margins, vascularity, calcifications, cervical lymph nodes). FNAC (fine needle aspiration cytology) — for nodules with suspicious US features (hypoechoic, irregular margins, microcalcifications, taller-than-wide shape, solid, >1 cm). Bethesda system for FNAC reporting (I-VI). Thyroid cancers: Papillary thyroid cancer (PTC, 80-85%): most common, excellent prognosis. Follicular thyroid cancer (FTC, 10%): vascular invasion, haematogenous spread. Medullary thyroid cancer (MTC, 5%): from

common, excellent prognosis. Follicular thyroid cancer (FTC, 10%): vascular invasion, haematogenous spread. Medullary thyroid cancer (MTC, 5%): from C-cells, produces calcitonin, associated with MEN2 syndrome. Anaplastic/undifferentiated: rare, extremely aggressive, median survival 3-5 months. Treatment: total thyroidectomy for most cancers >1 cm. Radioiodine ablation for PTC/FTC with residual disease or metastases. TSH suppression therapy (high-dose LT4 to keep TSH <0.1) for intermediate/high-risk DTC. Targeted therapy (vandetanib, cabozantinib for MTC; sorafenib, lenvatinib for iodine-refractory DTC). 8. Iodine Deficiency in India Iodine deficiency is the world's most prevalent, preventable cause of brain damage and intellectual disability. Iodine deficiency causes: goitre (most visible

is the world's most prevalent, preventable cause of brain damage and intellectual disability. Iodine deficiency causes: goitre (most visible manifestation), hypothyroidism, cretinism (congenital hypothyroidism — irreversible intellectual disability if untreated), increased risk of thyroid cancer. India had one of the world's largest iodine deficiency problems. Universal Salt Iodisation (USI) — mandatory iodization of edible salt (15-30 ppm iodine) — is the cornerstone of India's National Iodine Deficiency Disorders Control Programme (NIDDCP). India largely achieved iodine sufficiency in most states, though pockets remain in Himalayan regions and inland areas. Goitre belt: Sub-Himalayan belt stretching from Jammu and Kashmir through Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Jharkhand, West Bengal, and

regions and inland areas. Goitre belt: Sub-Himalayan belt stretching from Jammu and Kashmir through Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Jharkhand, West Bengal, and North-Eastern states. Iodine-poor soil → iodine-poor food and water. Regular monitoring of urinary iodine excretion (UIE) and goitre prevalence. 9. Thyroid in Pregnancy Pregnancy profoundly affects thyroid physiology: increased TBG (thyroxine-binding globulin), increased T4 demand, iodine loss via kidneys. TSH is suppressed in first trimester by hCG (cross-reacts with TSH receptors). Hypothyroidism in pregnancy: associated with miscarriage, pre-eclampsia, placental abruption, premature birth, low birth weight, impaired foetal neurodevelopment (IQ reduction). Treat promptly to maintain TSH <2.5 mIU/L in first trimester, <3 mIU/L later.

low birth weight, impaired foetal neurodevelopment (IQ reduction). Treat promptly to maintain TSH <2.5 mIU/L in first trimester, <3 mIU/L later. Hyperthyroidism in pregnancy: Graves' disease. PTU preferred in first trimester (methimazole associated with aplasia cutis, choanal atresia). Switch to methimazole/carbimazole in second trimester (PTU hepatotoxicity). Uncontrolled hyperthyroidism: foetal loss, preterm labour, small-for-gestational-age, neonatal thyrotoxicosis (maternal TRAb crosses placenta). Postpartum thyroiditis: affects 5-9% of women. Autoimmune thyroid inflammation post-partum. Transient hyperthyroid phase (1-4 months) followed by hypothyroid phase (4-8 months), usually recovering by 12 months. Some develop permanent hypothyroidism. 10. Thyroid Health — Screening and

by hypothyroid phase (4-8 months), usually recovering by 12 months. Some develop permanent hypothyroidism. 10. Thyroid Health — Screening and Awareness TSH testing is the single best screening test for thyroid dysfunction. Universal newborn TSH screening to detect congenital hypothyroidism (CH): TSH heel-prick on day 3-5 of life. Early LT4 treatment prevents permanent intellectual disability. India's Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK) includes thyroid screening. Opportunistic screening: women over 35, those with family history of autoimmune disease, patients on interferon, lithium, or amiodarone, people with unexplained anaemia, hyponatraemia, or elevated lipids. Awareness: thyroid conditions are significantly underdiagnosed in India, particularly in women. Campaigns by the Indian

or elevated lipids. Awareness: thyroid conditions are significantly underdiagnosed in India, particularly in women. Campaigns by the Indian Thyroid Society and public health initiatives increase awareness. Serum TSH cost has fallen significantly — widely available in most laboratories at ≤n200. Self-examination: patients should be taught neck self-examination. A rapidly enlarging nodule, hard texture, dysphonia (recurrent laryngeal nerve invasion), or cervical lymphadenopathy are red flags for thyroid cancer requiring urgent evaluation.

Tuberculosis (TB) — Comprehensive Guide 1. Overview and Global Burden Tuberculosis (TB) is a bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis (MTB), primarily affecting the lungs but capable of affecting virtually every organ system. TB remains the world's deadliest infectious disease from a single pathogen, responsible for approximately 1.6 million deaths annually. India carries the highest TB burden globally — 28% of the world's TB cases. In 2022, India notified approximately 2.4 million new TB cases. Despite significant progress, TB elimination (incidence <1 case per million population) remains a distant goal. India has set an ambitious target to eliminate TB by 2025 under the National Strategic Plan for TB Elimination, five years ahead of the WHO End TB Strategy's 2030

an ambitious target to eliminate TB by 2025 under the National Strategic Plan for TB Elimination, five years ahead of the WHO End TB Strategy's 2030 deadline. The Pradhan Mantri TB Mukt Bharat Abhiyaan (2022) involves community participation in supporting TB patients. 2. Microbiology and Transmission Mycobacterium tuberculosis is an aerobic, slow-growing, acid-fast bacillus (AFB). Its thick waxy cell wall (mycolic acid) makes it resistant to many antibiotics and the immune system. It can survive in dried sputum for weeks. Transmission: TB spreads primarily through the air when people with active pulmonary TB cough, sneeze, sing, or speak, releasing infectious aerosol droplet nuclei (1-5 micrometers) that can remain suspended for hours. A single cough can generate 3,000 infectious

releasing infectious aerosol droplet nuclei (1-5 micrometers) that can remain suspended for hours. A single cough can generate 3,000 infectious particles. Risk of infection depends on: proximity and duration of exposure, infectiousness of the source case (sputum smear-positive cases are most infectious), ventilation of the shared space, and susceptibility of the exposed individual (HIV, malnutrition, diabetes, immunosuppression markedly increase risk). 3. Latent vs Active TB After inhaling MTB, the immune system usually contains the bacteria: granulomas form and the bacteria enter a latent state. Latent TB infection (LTBI): bacteria dormant, person not sick, not infectious, tuberculin skin test (TST) or IGRA positive. Lifetime risk of LTBI reactivating to active TB is approximately 10%

person not sick, not infectious, tuberculin skin test (TST) or IGRA positive. Lifetime risk of LTBI reactivating to active TB is approximately 10% (5% in first 2 years, 5% over lifetime) in immunocompetent individuals. In HIV-positive individuals, the annual risk is 5-10%. Active TB: bacteria multiply, person is sick and (if pulmonary) infectious. Factors promoting reactivation: HIV infection (most important), malnutrition, diabetes (2-3x risk), silicosis, end-stage renal disease, immunosuppressive therapy, alcohol abuse, smoking, and extreme age. 4. Symptoms and Extrapulmonary TB Pulmonary TB (most common form, 60-70%): persistent productive cough (>2 weeks), haemoptysis, chest pain, fever (often low-grade, evening rise), drenching night sweats, unexplained weight loss, fatigue, and

cough (>2 weeks), haemoptysis, chest pain, fever (often low-grade, evening rise), drenching night sweats, unexplained weight loss, fatigue, and anorexia. Advanced disease: breathlessness, formation of cavities. Extrapulmonary TB (30-40%): Lymph node TB (most common extrapulmonary form in India) — painless swelling of cervical nodes. Pleural TB — pleural effusion with exudative fluid. Bone and joint TB — Pott's disease (spinal TB causing gibbus deformity, cord compression). TB Meningitis (TBM) — severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, confusion, cranial nerve palsies, hydrocephalus. Very high mortality and morbidity. CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, high protein, low glucose, AFB smear/culture/Xpert. Abdominal TB — peritonitis (ascites with insidious onset), intestinal TB (ileocaecal

pleocytosis, high protein, low glucose, AFB smear/culture/Xpert. Abdominal TB — peritonitis (ascites with insidious onset), intestinal TB (ileocaecal region, causing obstruction, malabsorption), hepatic and splenic involvement. Miliary TB — haematogenous dissemination to all organs; minute bilateral granulomas on chest X-ray (millet seed pattern). 5. Diagnosis Sputum smear microscopy (AFB smear): inexpensive, rapid, widely available. Detects MTB in sputum. Sensitivity 45-80% (lower in HIV). Requires 2 sputum samples (spot and early morning). Xpert MTB/RIF assay (GeneXpert): molecular test (PCR-based). 90-minute turnaround. Sensitivity ~89% vs culture as reference standard, near-100% specificity. Simultaneously detects rifampicin resistance. Now available in most district TB centres under

culture as reference standard, near-100% specificity. Simultaneously detects rifampicin resistance. Now available in most district TB centres under NTEP. Sputum culture (MGIT Liquid culture or LJ solid medium): gold standard. Higher sensitivity. MGIT culture takes 1-6 weeks. Essential for DST (drug susceptibility testing) in resistant TB. Chest X-ray: characteristic findings — upper lobe infiltrates, cavities, hilar lymphadenopathy, miliary pattern. Not diagnostic alone. Normal CXR does not exclude extrapulmonary TB. IGRA (Interferon-Gamma Release Assay, e.g. QuantiFERON-TB Gold): blood test for LTBI. More specific than TST (not affected by BCG vaccination). Not diagnostic of active TB. 6. Drug-Resistant TB MDR-TB (Multi-drug resistant TB): resistant to at least isoniazid AND rifampicin — the two most potent first-line drugs. In India, approximately 6% of new TB cases and 11-13% of previously treated cases are MDR-TB. XDR-TB (Extensively drug-resistant TB): older definition — MDR + fluoroquinolone + injectable resistance. Newer WHO 2021 definition — MDR/RR-TB + resistance to any fluoroquinolone. Pre-XDR-TB is now defined. Treatment: BPaL regimen —

resistance. Newer WHO 2021 definition — MDR/RR-TB + resistance to any fluoroquinolone. Pre-XDR-TB is now defined. Treatment: BPaL regimen — Bedaquiline + Pretomanid + Linezolid ± Moxifloxacin for 6 months (TB-PRACTECAL, ZeNix, NExT trials). This represents a revolution in MDR-TB treatment, replacing 18-24 month regimens with injections with shorter all-oral regimens with fewer side effects. Key side effects to monitor: bedaquiline — QT prolongation (ECG monitoring monthly), hepatotoxicity; linezolid — peripheral neuropathy, optic neuritis, myelosuppression (CBC monthly); delamanid — QT prolongation. 8. Diagnosis of TB in PLHIV is challenging — atypical presentations, lower rates of cavitation, sputum smear often negative, and extrapulmonary TB much more common. Urine LAM (lipoarabinomannan) test is useful for bedside rapid diagnosis in PLHIV with low CD4. Treatment: treat TB first, start ART 2-8 weeks later (except TB meningitis — defer ART 4-6 weeks). Rifampicin is a potent inducer of cytochrome P450 and significantly reduces levels of many antiretroviral drugs — dose adjustment of efavirenz is required; rifabutin is an alternative in some regimens. Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome (IRIS): paradoxical worsening of TB symptoms after ART initiation due to restored immune response. Treat with NSAIDs or corticosteroids. Usually self-limiting. 9. TB in Special Populations and Infection Control TB in children: presents more commonly as extrapulmonary or disseminated TB. Bacteriological confirmation harder (paucibacillary disease). Clinical algorithm (symptoms + contact history + X-ray + TST/IGRA) used. Xpert Ultra improves yield. BCG vaccination at birth offers 70-80% protection against severe forms (miliary, meningitis) in children. TB in pregnancy: rifampicin and isoniazid are safe throughout pregnancy. Streptomycin absolutely contraindicated (causes fetal ototoxicity). TB in pregnancy associated with higher maternal and neonatal mortality. Treat without delay. Infection control: administrative controls (triage, fast-tracking, separation of infectious patients, cough hygiene). Environmental controls (natural and mechanical ventilation — 12+ air changes per hour in TB wards; UV germicidal irradiation). Personal protective equipment (N95 respirators for HCWs — NOT surgical masks). Healthcare worker TB: HCWs have 2-5x higher TB risk than the general population. Nikshay, NTEP, and the Path to Elimination Nikshay is India's web-based TB patient management system under NTEP. All TB patients must be notified on Nikshay. The Nikshay Poshan Yojana provides Rs 500/month nutritional support to TB patients for the duration of treatment. The NTEP uses Directly Observed Treatment Short-course (DOTS) to ensure treatment adherence. Community volunteers (Nikshay Mitras) provide support and monitoring. Video-Observed Therapy (VOT) is being scaled up. Key challenges: catastrophic costs (TB pushes ~50 million Indians into poverty annually), stigma, nutritional deficiency (TB prevalence is highest in

Key challenges: catastrophic costs (TB pushes ~50 million Indians into poverty annually), stigma, nutritional deficiency (TB prevalence is highest in malnourished populations — BMI <18.5 doubles TB risk), access in tribal and hard-to-reach areas, and missing the private sector (60% of TB patients first seek care in the private sector in India). India's path to TB elimination requires: universal drug susceptibility testing, accelerated scale-up of preventive therapy, addressing social determinants (nutrition, poverty, housing), active case-finding campaigns, and engaging the private sector under the 99DOTS and private provider engagement initiatives.
